meta.title: SF-SGI-1: Cortex AI, Search, and Responsible GenAI

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability lets analysts call large language models directly from SQL without moving data out of Snowflake?

options.text: Snowpark Container Services
Snowflake Cortex AISQL functions such as `COMPLETE`
Dynamic tables
Secure data sharing

questions.explanation: `COMPLETE` and other Cortex AISQL functions let you invoke supported models directly from SQL inside Snowflake. The other options are useful Snowflake features, but they are not the core SQL LLM invocation surface.

questions.stem: A team wants short summaries of support tickets stored in a table. Which SQL function is purpose-built for this task?

options.text: `SUMMARIZE`
`TRANSLATE`
`SEARCH_PREVIEW`
`COUNT_TOKENS`

questions.explanation: `SUMMARIZE` is the task-specific Cortex function for generating summaries. `TRANSLATE` changes language, `SEARCH_PREVIEW` is not the summarization interface, and token counting does not generate text.

questions.stem: A developer is preparing a `COMPLETE` call that uses chat-style messages. Which TWO elements are valid message components? (Select TWO)

options.text: A role such as `system` or `user`
An array containing structured message objects
A mandatory warehouse size parameter inside the message body
A required embedding vector attached to every message

questions.explanation: `COMPLETE` supports chat-style inputs with role/content message objects, including system and user roles. Warehouse sizing is configured outside the message payload, and embedding vectors are not required for every chat message.

questions.stem: A team needs deterministic extraction of invoice fields and wants to minimize creative variation in model output. Which change is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Increase temperature and top_p
Lower temperature and constrain the prompt to the required fields
Switch to a larger warehouse only
Replace SQL calls with manual annotation

questions.explanation: Lower temperature reduces randomness and is better for extraction-style tasks. Prompting for only the required fields further narrows the output. Larger warehouses affect compute, not generation behavior, and manual annotation does not solve inference determinism.

questions.stem: Why would a team use `EMBED_TEXT_768` or `EMBED_TEXT_1024` in a GenAI pipeline?

options.text: To create vector representations for semantic search or similarity tasks
To generate a final natural-language answer for the user
To create a virtual warehouse
To enforce row access policies

questions.explanation: Embedding functions convert text into vectors used for semantic retrieval, similarity, and RAG indexing. They do not generate final answers, provision compute, or apply governance policies.

questions.stem: What is a practical reason to choose a task-specific Cortex function such as `CLASSIFY_TEXT` instead of a generic `COMPLETE` prompt?

options.text: It is typically simpler and more targeted for the specific task
It bypasses Snowflake role-based access control
It avoids warehouse usage entirely
It guarantees zero hallucinations

questions.explanation: Task-specific functions are easier to use for common tasks and reduce prompt engineering overhead. They do not bypass access control, eliminate compute needs, or guarantee perfect outputs.

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to compare prompt revisions for sentiment extraction. Which approach is MOST aligned with Snowflake usage?

options.text: Store prompt versions and compare outputs on a representative evaluation set
Use only the newest prompt because evaluation is unnecessary
Change warehouses between runs and treat cost changes as quality signals
Disable SQL history to avoid noisy comparisons

questions.explanation: Comparing prompt versions on representative examples is the right evaluation pattern. Warehouse cost is not a direct quality metric, and disabling history removes auditability rather than improving evaluation.

questions.stem: A team calls `COMPLETE` on a table of 10 million rows and sees slow end-to-end execution. Which action is MOST likely to improve throughput first?

options.text: Process rows in larger, well-planned batches instead of many tiny ad hoc calls
Move all data to a client laptop and call external APIs there
Replace every LLM call with a dynamic table
Disable result caching for the session

questions.explanation: Large GenAI workloads benefit from efficient batching and set-oriented execution patterns. Pulling data out of Snowflake adds movement and risk, dynamic tables do not replace inference, and disabling caching is not the primary throughput lever here.

questions.stem: What is Snowflake Cortex Search designed to provide?

options.text: Semantic and hybrid retrieval over indexed text data
Automatic warehouse resizing for SQL queries
A replacement for Snowflake roles
Low-level GPU management

questions.explanation: Cortex Search provides semantic retrieval over indexed content and supports hybrid retrieval patterns. It is not a warehouse feature, a security model, or an infrastructure management tool.

questions.stem: A team wants newly inserted product documents to become searchable with minimal custom synchronization code. Which design is BEST?

options.text: Create a Cortex Search service on the source data and let Snowflake maintain the search index
Export the documents nightly to a spreadsheet
Run manual vector calculations in every analyst notebook
Use only metadata filters without any indexed content

questions.explanation: A Cortex Search service is the managed retrieval mechanism and reduces custom synchronization work. The other options either add manual steps or remove the semantic search capability.

questions.stem: A RAG application on Snowflake typically includes which TWO stages? (Select TWO)

options.text: Retrieve relevant context from a search service
Assemble retrieved context into the generation prompt
Resize micro-partitions manually before every query
Disable warehouse auto-suspend during answer generation

questions.explanation: RAG combines retrieval with prompt assembly and generation. Manual micro-partition resizing and auto-suspend changes are not core RAG stages.

questions.stem: Users report that search results often contain loosely related marketing pages instead of policy manuals. Which remediation is MOST targeted?

options.text: Improve chunking and indexed source selection so the search corpus contains cleaner, task-relevant text
Increase the number of user roles with access to the service
Lower warehouse auto-suspend to 1 minute
Convert the policy manuals to JPEG images only

questions.explanation: Poor corpus quality and chunking frequently degrade retrieval quality. Access changes and suspend settings do not improve relevance, and image-only storage makes semantic retrieval harder.

questions.stem: Why is metadata filtering valuable in enterprise retrieval pipelines?

options.text: It narrows retrieved results to documents that match business constraints such as region or product line
It makes the model ignore the user prompt
It replaces the need for chunking
It guarantees exact-match SQL semantics

questions.explanation: Metadata filtering improves precision by restricting candidate documents using structured attributes. It complements, rather than replaces, chunking and semantic retrieval.

questions.stem: A chatbot must answer only from approved HR policies. What is the BEST retrieval design?

options.text: Index only the approved HR corpus and apply metadata filters where appropriate
Index the entire internet and trust the model to choose wisely
Skip retrieval and rely on model pretraining only
Return random top-k documents to avoid bias

questions.explanation: Constraining the searchable corpus to approved HR documents is the strongest way to reduce irrelevant or unsafe context. Broader corpora and no-retrieval designs increase risk.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of document chunking before retrieval?

options.text: To split content into smaller units that can be indexed and retrieved more precisely
To encrypt all text at rest
To create new Snowflake roles automatically
To reduce the number of databases in an account

questions.explanation: Chunking helps retrieval return focused passages instead of entire long documents. It does not manage encryption, RBAC, or account organization.

questions.stem: A team wants to improve answer faithfulness in a Snowflake RAG app. Which change is MOST likely to help?

options.text: Pass the retrieved passages into the final prompt and instruct the model to answer from that context
Remove retrieval and ask the model to rely on general world knowledge
Increase temperature so the model explores more answers
Store the retrieved passages in a hidden worksheet only

questions.explanation: Faithfulness improves when the model is grounded in retrieved context and instructed to use it. Removing retrieval or increasing randomness usually worsens grounding.

questions.stem: What is Snowflake Document AI primarily used for?

options.text: Extracting structured information from unstructured documents such as invoices or forms
Creating Snowpark UDF packages
Scheduling tasks
Configuring replication groups

questions.explanation: Document AI is for extracting structured information from documents. The other options are unrelated platform capabilities.

questions.stem: A team wants to process scanned invoices before downstream analytics. Which sequence is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Stage the documents, run Document AI extraction, then load extracted fields into analytic tables
Convert every invoice to CSV manually first
Create a stream on the PDF bytes and query them directly with SQL joins
Skip extraction and ask analysts to read the images

questions.explanation: Document AI is designed to extract fields from staged unstructured documents so the results can be stored in structured tables. The alternatives are manual or technically misaligned.

questions.stem: Which TWO data-preparation practices improve downstream GenAI quality? (Select TWO)

options.text: Remove repeated boilerplate and navigation text before indexing
Preserve useful metadata such as source, date, or business unit
Duplicate every chunk five times to increase recall artificially
Randomly shuffle sentences inside each chunk

questions.explanation: Cleaning boilerplate and preserving metadata both improve retrieval quality and filtering. Duplicating chunks and scrambling sentences generally add noise.

questions.stem: A search index contains many near-duplicate policy documents from multiple yearly exports. What is the biggest risk if the duplicates are left in place?

options.text: Retrieval results may become repetitive and crowd out more useful passages
Snowflake role hierarchy stops working
Virtual warehouses cannot auto-suspend
SQL joins become impossible

questions.explanation: Near-duplicate documents can dominate top results and reduce retrieval diversity. They do not break RBAC, auto-suspend, or basic SQL behavior.

questions.stem: Why is preserving document lineage important in a GenAI workflow?

options.text: It helps teams trace an answer back to its source and supports auditability
It guarantees the model will choose the cheapest warehouse
It eliminates the need for validation
It replaces role-based access control

questions.explanation: Source lineage supports trust, debugging, and auditability. It does not manage cost automatically, remove validation needs, or replace RBAC.

questions.stem: A team is creating chunks for long policy manuals. Which chunking objective is BEST?

options.text: Keep each chunk semantically coherent so retrieval returns meaningful context
Maximize chunk size until each chunk equals the full manual
Split only on random character counts with no regard for section boundaries
Store only document titles

questions.explanation: Semantically coherent chunks usually improve retrieval precision and answer quality. Oversized or random chunks reduce relevance, and titles alone are insufficient context.

questions.stem: Which input characteristic most strongly determines whether Document AI can extract value from a file?

options.text: Whether the document contains interpretable text or recognizable document structure
Whether the file is stored in a larger warehouse
Whether the account has many databases
Whether the session uses a temporary table

questions.explanation: Document AI depends on usable document content and structure. Warehouse size, database count, and temporary tables do not determine whether extraction is possible.

questions.stem: A team wants a multilingual knowledge base. Which preprocessing step is MOST important before embedding content from many languages?

options.text: Use a consistent strategy for language handling so retrieved chunks and user queries are comparable
Turn every paragraph into uppercase text only
Remove all metadata because metadata can bias search
Store images instead of text whenever possible

questions.explanation: Multilingual systems need a deliberate language strategy so retrieval and prompting remain comparable and understandable. The other choices either remove useful information or degrade search quality.

questions.stem: What is the primary goal of GenAI evaluation in production?

options.text: Measure whether outputs are useful, grounded, and safe for the intended use case
Maximize the number of open worksheets
Increase the number of schemas in the account
Avoid keeping any query history

questions.explanation: Evaluation checks whether the application actually meets quality and safety goals. It is not about worksheet volume, schema count, or removing audit trails.

questions.stem: A regulated team wants to know who ran GenAI queries and when. Which Snowflake capability helps most?

options.text: Query history and account usage views for auditing activity
Dynamic table lag metrics only
Automatic clustering
Fail-safe retention alone

questions.explanation: Snowflake audit and query history capabilities help teams inspect who executed queries and when. The other options do not provide the same action-level audit signal.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices best support trustworthy enterprise GenAI rollouts? (Select TWO)

options.text: Evaluate answers on representative business tasks before broad release
Capture source references or lineage for generated answers
Rely on one demo prompt and skip further testing
Grant every user unrestricted access to all models by default

questions.explanation: Representative evaluation and source traceability both strengthen trust and governance. One-off demos and overly broad access increase risk.

questions.stem: A legal team says generated answers must stay within approved source material. What is the MOST defensible control?

options.text: Ground answers on retrieved approved content and test for unsupported claims
Increase temperature because more diversity means more compliance
Disable all warehouses except one
Require analysts to memorize every source document

questions.explanation: Grounding on approved content plus evaluation for unsupported claims is the strongest control among the options. Temperature and warehouse count do not enforce factual scope.

questions.stem: Why should a team keep a small holdout set of prompts when iterating on prompts and retrieval?

options.text: To detect whether changes improve general quality rather than only a few hand-picked examples
To reduce Snowflake storage costs to zero
To replace role-based access control
To avoid using any official documentation

questions.explanation: A holdout set helps prevent overfitting to a tiny sample of examples. It does not eliminate storage cost, replace RBAC, or remove the need for documentation.

questions.stem: Which metric is MOST useful when checking whether a RAG answer used the right supporting context?

options.text: A relevance or grounding-oriented review against retrieved passages
The number of databases in the account
Warehouse auto-suspend duration
The count of secure shares

questions.explanation: Grounding or relevance checks compare the answer against retrieved evidence. Database count, auto-suspend, and shares do not measure answer quality.

questions.stem: Why should sensitive fields be protected before being sent to a GenAI function?

options.text: To reduce the chance of exposing confidential information in prompts or outputs
To increase micro-partition count
To disable SQL compilation
To force all users onto the ACCOUNTADMIN role

questions.explanation: Sensitive data should be protected so prompts and outputs do not leak confidential information. The other options are unrelated or unsafe.

questions.stem: A company wants only a limited set of roles to use Cortex features. Which control is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Use Snowflake role-based access control and grant access only to approved roles
Allow all roles to use the features and rely on naming conventions
Move all prompts into comments
Create more warehouses

questions.explanation: RBAC is the core enterprise control for limiting feature access. Naming conventions and warehouses do not enforce authorization.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices reduce the risk of harmful or irrelevant GenAI outputs? (Select TWO)

options.text: Constrain prompts with clear instructions and approved context
Review outputs on representative edge cases before production rollout
Increase randomness without testing
Skip access reviews because the model is managed

questions.explanation: Prompt constraints and edge-case testing both reduce risk. Untested randomness and skipped access reviews do the opposite.

questions.stem: A team stores customer support transcripts and wants to build a chatbot. Which design best balances usefulness and privacy?

options.text: Redact or mask sensitive fields where possible and restrict retrieval to the minimum necessary corpus
Expose the entire raw transcript archive to every employee
Disable all logging so no one can review misuse
Move data to personal laptops for experimentation

questions.explanation: Masking/redaction plus least-privilege retrieval balances utility and privacy. Broad exposure, zero logging, and unmanaged local copies increase risk.

questions.stem: Why is least-privilege access especially important for enterprise GenAI apps?

options.text: Because retrieved context may include business data that many users should not see
Because it makes tokenization mathematically impossible
Because warehouses cannot run without ACCOUNTADMIN
Because it removes the need for monitoring

questions.explanation: Least privilege limits exposure of underlying data and generated outputs. It does not change tokenization rules, warehouse requirements, or monitoring needs.

questions.stem: What is a good first response when users report that the chatbot occasionally fabricates policy details?

options.text: Add evaluation cases for those failures and tighten grounding instructions around approved sources
Disable all search services permanently
Increase output length so the answers look more confident
Grant broader access to raw tables

questions.explanation: Hallucination reports should feed back into evaluation and grounding improvements. The other actions either ignore the root cause or increase risk.

questions.stem: Which statement about responsible AI governance is MOST accurate?

options.text: It is an ongoing process that includes access control, evaluation, and monitoring
It ends once the first prompt works in a demo
It is only about warehouse cost
It applies only to external APIs and not in-platform AI

questions.explanation: Responsible AI governance is continuous and includes controls, evaluation, and monitoring. It does not end after a demo and is not limited to cost or external APIs.

questions.stem: A business unit wants a model to answer questions outside the indexed corpus using its general knowledge. For a compliance assistant, what is the strongest objection?

options.text: It weakens traceability and increases the chance of unsupported answers in a regulated workflow
It makes warehouses cheaper
It guarantees higher factual accuracy
It removes the need for role-based access control

questions.explanation: Letting the model answer beyond approved sources reduces traceability and can create unsupported claims, which is risky in compliance settings. The other statements are incorrect.

questions.stem: A team is designing a review process for high-impact generated outputs. Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Require human review for high-risk cases while automating lower-risk flows
Eliminate all monitoring once prompts look stable
Approve outputs only by warehouse size
Use random approvals with no policy

questions.explanation: Human review for high-risk outputs is a practical governance pattern. The other approaches do not meaningfully control risk.

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of keeping audit-friendly records for GenAI experiments and deployments?

options.text: Teams can explain decisions, investigate issues, and demonstrate governance later
It eliminates the need for source data
It makes every prompt universally correct
It prevents all future model changes

questions.explanation: Audit-friendly records support explainability, debugging, and compliance. They do not remove source-data requirements, make prompts perfect, or freeze future changes.
